revision.text:
'''Shizuoka Sengen Shrine'''(静岡浅間神社) is a [[Jinja (shrine)|Shinto Shrine]] in [[Shizuoka]], [[Japan]]. To be exact, Sizuoka Sengen Shrine is a collective term of three shrines, Kanbe Shrine(神部神社), Asama Shrine(浅間神社), Ohtoshimioya Shrine(大歳御祖神社).
*Kanbe Shrine, established 2100 years ago, enshrined a soul of Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto who was a divinity of [[Suruga Province]]. *Sengen Shrine enshrined Konohanasakuya-hime and was built in 901 by a command of [[Emperor Daigo]]. *Ohtoshimioya Shrine was established 1700 years ago and worships Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto as a god.
{{japan-struct-stub}}
[[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shizuoka]]
[[ja:静岡浅間神社]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Shizuoka Sengen Jinja
静岡浅間神社 | image = Shizuoka Sengen Shrine Ohaiden.jpg | caption = Prayer Hall of Shizuoka Sengen Jinja | type = Asama shrine | dedication = [[Ookuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Konohanasakuya-hime]],
[[Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto]] | founded = | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = 102-1 Miyagasaki-chō, Aoi-ku, Shizuoka, 420-0868 | phone = 054-245-1820 | website = http://www.shizuokasengen.net/ }} {{nihongo|'''Shizuoka Sengen Jinja'''|静岡浅間神社}} is the name for a collective group of three [[Shinto shrine]]s now forming a single religous corporation, located at Mount Shizuhata in [[Aoi-ku, Shizuoka]] in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. These shrines are the Kambe Jinja (神部神社), Sengen Jinja (浅間神社), and Ohtoshimioya Jinja (大歳御祖神社). The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 5. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Kambe Jinja is the [[Ookuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]], who is regarded as the mythical founding deity of [[Suruga Province]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Sengen Jinja is the [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the deity of [[Mount Fuji]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Ohtoshimioya Shrine is the [[Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto]], who appears in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as a daughter of [[Susano-o]], and a ''kami'' protecting markets and commerce. ==History== The date of the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja’s foundation is unknown. The area has been inhabited since prehistoric times, and a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mound]] has been excavated at [[Shizuhatagayama Kofun|Mount Shizuhata]]. Per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', the area was colonized by the [[Hata clan]] during this period. According to unsubstantiated shrine legend, the foundation of the Kambe Jinja dates to the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]], that of the Ohtoshimioya Shrine to the reign of [[Emperor Ojin]], both from the Kofun period. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, Kambe Jinja was given national recognition and status of the ''Sōja'' of Suruga Province in the [[Heian period]]. Also, the date of 901 is given for the foundation of the Sengen Jinja, as a subsidiary branch of the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], and initially was referred to as the “Shingu” (new shrine). Through the [[Kamakura period|Kamakura]] and [[Muromachi period]]s, the shrines enjoyed the patronage of the powerful warrior clans who dominated the Suruga area: the [[Minamoto clan]], [[Hojo clan]], [[Imagawa clan]], [[Takeda clan]] and the [[Tokugawa clan]]. In particular, the first Tokugawa [[Shōgun]], [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], sponsored the rebuilding of the shrines after his retirement to nearby [[Sumpu Castle]], and subsequent Shogun continued to worship at the shrines throughout the [[Edo period]]. The 3rd Shōgun, [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] granted the shrines lands with 2313 [[koku]] in revenue for their upkeep. However, the shrine complex burned down in a fire of 1804. It was rebuilt over a 60 year period at a cost of over 100,000 gold ''[[ryō]]'' by the [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] in the its flamboyant [[Momoyama period|Momoyama style]], with extensive use of [[lacquer]], wood carvings, and [[gold leaf]]. Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as Important Cultural Properties]], forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. ==Subsidiary shrines== In addition to the three main shrines, the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja complex also has four subsidiary shrines: * Hayama Jinja (麓山神社) dedicated to [[Ohoyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and to [[Yamato-takeru]]. It was founded in 1878 * Yachiho Jinja (八千戈神社), an amalgamation of 18 small shrines and 13 small chapels found in the surrounding area. It was founded in 1873. * Sukunahiko Jinja (少彦名神社), formerly the [[Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi-do]] of Kambe Jinja, turned into a shrine in Meiji period due to [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Buddhism from Shinto]]. * Tamahoko Jinja (玉鉾神) dedicated to the four main Edo period ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholars. ==Cultural properties== Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as Important Cultural Properties (ICP), forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine’s non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) ISBN 1873410638 ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{coord|35|07|19|N|138|55|08|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shizuoka]] [[it:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]] [[ja:静岡浅間神社]]

The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Sengen Jinja is the [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the deity of [[Mount Fuji]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Ohtoshimioya Shrine is the [[Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto]], who appears in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as a daughter of [[Susano-o]], and a ''kami'' protecting markets and commerce. ==History== The date of the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja’s foundation is unknown. The area has been inhabited since prehistoric times, and a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mound]] has been excavated at [[Shizuhatagayama Kofun|Mount Shizuhata]]. Per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', the area was colonized by the [[Hata clan]] during this period. According to unsubstantiated shrine legend, the foundation of the Kambe Jinja dates to the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]], that of the Ohtoshimioya Shrine to the reign of [[Emperor Ojin]], both from the Kofun period. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, Kambe Jinja was given national recognition and status of the ''Sōja'' of Suruga Province in the [[Heian period]]. Also, the date of 901 is given for the foundation of the Sengen Jinja, as a subsidiary branch of the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], and initially was referred to as the “Shingu” (new shrine). Through the [[Kamakura period|Kamakura]] and [[Muromachi period]]s, the shrines enjoyed the patronage of the powerful warrior clans who dominated the Suruga area: the [[Minamoto clan]], [[Hojo clan]], [[Imagawa clan]], [[Takeda clan]] and the [[Tokugawa clan]]. In particular, the first Tokugawa [[Shōgun]], [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], sponsored the rebuilding of the shrines after his retirement to nearby [[Sumpu Castle]], and subsequent Shogun continued to worship at the shrines throughout the [[Edo period]]. The 3rd Shōgun, [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] granted the shrines lands with 2313 [[koku]] in revenue for their upkeep. However, the shrine complex burned down in a fire of 1804. It was rebuilt over a 60 year period at a cost of over 100,000 gold ''[[ryō]]'' by the [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] in the its flamboyant [[Momoyama period|Momoyama style]], with extensive use of [[lacquer]], wood carvings, and [[gold leaf]]. Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as Important Cultural Properties]], forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. ==Subsidiary shrines== In addition to the three main shrines, the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja complex also has four subsidiary shrines: * Hayama Jinja (麓山神社) dedicated to [[Ohoyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and to [[Yamato-takeru]]. It was founded in 1878 * Yachiho Jinja (八千戈神社), an amalgamation of 18 small shrines and 13 small chapels found in the surrounding area. It was founded in 1873. * Sukunahiko Jinja (少彦名神社), formerly the [[Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi-do]] of Kambe Jinja, turned into a shrine in Meiji period due to [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Buddhism from Shinto]]. * Tamahoko Jinja (玉鉾神) dedicated to the four main Edo period ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholars. ==Cultural properties== Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as Important Cultural Properties (ICP), forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine’s non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) ISBN 1873410638 ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{coord|35|07|19|N|138|55|08|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shizuoka]] [[it:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]] [[ja:静岡浅間神社]]

The area has been inhabited since prehistoric times, and a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mound]] has been excavated at [[Shizuhatagayama Kofun|Mount Shizuhata]]. Per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', the area was colonized by the [[Hata clan]] during this period. According to unsubstantiated shrine legend, the foundation of the Kambe Jinja dates to the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]], that of the Ohtoshimioya Shrine to the reign of [[Emperor Ojin]], both from the Kofun period. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, Kambe Jinja was given national recognition and status of the ''Sōja'' of Suruga Province in the [[Heian period]]. Also, the date of 901 is given for the foundation of the Sengen Jinja, as a subsidiary branch of the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], and initially was referred to as the "Shingu" (new shrine). Through the [[Kamakura period|Kamakura]] and [[Muromachi period]]s, the shrines enjoyed the patronage of the powerful warrior clans who dominated the Suruga area: the [[Minamoto clan]], [[Hojo clan]], [[Imagawa clan]], [[Takeda clan]] and the [[Tokugawa clan]]. In particular, the first Tokugawa [[Shōgun]], [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], sponsored the rebuilding of the shrines after his retirement to nearby [[Sumpu Castle]], and subsequent Shogun continued to worship at the shrines throughout the [[Edo period]]. The 3rd Shōgun, [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] granted the shrines lands with 2313 [[koku]] in revenue for their upkeep. However, the shrine complex burned down in a fire of 1804. It was rebuilt over a 60 year period at a cost of over 100,000 gold ''[[ryō]]'' by the [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] in the its flamboyant [[Momoyama period|Momoyama style]], with extensive use of [[lacquer]], wood carvings, and [[gold leaf]]. Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]], forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. ==Subsidiary shrines== In addition to the three main shrines, the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja complex also has four subsidiary shrines: * Hayama Jinja (麓山神社) dedicated to [[Ohoyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and to [[Yamato-takeru]]. It was founded in 1878 * Yachiho Jinja (八千戈神社), an amalgamation of 18 small shrines and 13 small chapels found in the surrounding area. It was founded in 1873. * Sukunahiko Jinja (少彦名神社), formerly the [[Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi-do]] of Kambe Jinja, turned into a shrine in Meiji period due to [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Buddhism from Shinto]]. * Tamahoko Jinja (玉鉾神) dedicated to the four main Edo period ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholars. ==Cultural properties== Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]] (ICP), forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine's non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. These shrines are the Kambe Jinja (神部神社), Sengen Jinja (浅間神社), and Ohtoshimioya Jinja (大歳御祖神社). The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 5. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Kambe Jinja is the [[Ookuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]], who is regarded as the mythical founding deity of [[Suruga Province]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Sengen Jinja is the [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the deity of [[Mount Fuji]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Ohtoshimioya Shrine is the [[Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto]], who appears in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as a daughter of [[Susano-o]], and a ''kami'' protecting markets and commerce. ==History== The date of the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja's foundation is unknown. The area has been inhabited since prehistoric times, and a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mound]] has been excavated at [[Shizuhatagayama Kofun|Mount Shizuhata]]. Per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', the area was colonized by the [[Hata clan]] during this period. According to unsubstantiated shrine legend, the foundation of the Kambe Jinja dates to the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]], that of the Ohtoshimioya Shrine to the reign of [[Emperor Ojin]], both from the Kofun period. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, Kambe Jinja was given national recognition and status of the ''Sōja'' of Suruga Province in the [[Heian period]]. Also, the date of 901 is given for the foundation of the Sengen Jinja, as a subsidiary branch of the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], and initially was referred to as the "Shingu" (new shrine). Through the [[Kamakura period|Kamakura]] and [[Muromachi period]]s, the shrines enjoyed the patronage of the powerful warrior clans who dominated the Suruga area: the [[Minamoto clan]], [[Hojo clan]], [[Imagawa clan]], [[Takeda clan]] and the [[Tokugawa clan]]. In particular, the first Tokugawa [[Shōgun]], [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], sponsored the rebuilding of the shrines after his retirement to nearby [[Sumpu Castle]], and subsequent Shogun continued to worship at the shrines throughout the [[Edo period]]. The 3rd Shōgun, [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] granted the shrines lands with 2313 [[koku]] in revenue for their upkeep. However, the shrine complex burned down in a fire of 1804. It was rebuilt over a 60 year period at a cost of over 100,000 gold ''[[ryō]]'' by the [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] in the its flamboyant [[Momoyama period|Momoyama style]], with extensive use of [[lacquer]], wood carvings, and [[gold leaf]]. Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]], forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. In the [[modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], Shizuoka Sengen was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Shōsha''|国幣小社}}. ==Subsidiary shrines== In addition to the three main shrines, the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja complex also has four subsidiary shrines: * Hayama Jinja (麓山神社) dedicated to [[Ohoyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and to [[Yamato-takeru]]. It was founded in 1878 * Yachiho Jinja (八千戈神社), an amalgamation of 18 small shrines and 13 small chapels found in the surrounding area. It was founded in 1873. * Sukunahiko Jinja (少彦名神社), formerly the [[Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi-do]] of Kambe Jinja, turned into a shrine in Meiji period due to [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Buddhism from Shinto]]. * Tamahoko Jinja (玉鉾神) dedicated to the four main Edo period ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholars. ==Cultural properties== Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]] (ICP), forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine's non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) ISBN 1-873410-63-8 {{coord|35|07|19|N|138|55|08|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shizuoka City]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Shizuoka Sengen Jinja
静岡浅間神社 | image = Shizuoka Sengen Shrine Ohaiden.jpg | caption = Prayer Hall of Shizuoka Sengen Jinja | type = Asama shrine | dedication = [[Ookuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Konohanasakuya-hime]],
[[Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto]] | founded = | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = 102-1 Miyagasaki-chō, Aoi-ku, Shizuoka, 420-0868 | phone = 054-245-1820 | website = http://www.shizuokasengen.net/ }} {{nihongo|'''Shizuoka Sengen Jinja'''|静岡浅間神社}} is the name for a collective group of three [[Shinto shrine]]s now forming a single religious corporation, located at Mount Shizuhata in [[Aoi-ku, Shizuoka]] in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. These shrines are the Kambe Jinja (神部神社), Sengen Jinja (浅間神社), and Ohtoshimioya Jinja (大歳御祖神社). The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 5. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Kambe Jinja is the [[Ookuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]], who is regarded as the mythical founding deity of [[Suruga Province]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Sengen Jinja is the [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the deity of [[Mount Fuji]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Ohtoshimioya Shrine is the [[Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto]], who appears in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as a daughter of [[Susano-o]], and a ''kami'' protecting markets and commerce. ==History== The date of the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja's foundation is unknown. The area has been inhabited since prehistoric times, and a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mound]] has been excavated at [[Shizuhatagayama Kofun|Mount Shizuhata]]. Per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', the area was colonized by the [[Hata clan]] during this period. According to unsubstantiated shrine legend, the foundation of the Kambe Jinja dates to the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]], that of the Ohtoshimioya Shrine to the reign of [[Emperor Ōjin]], both from the Kofun period. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, Kambe Jinja was given national recognition and status of the ''Sōja'' of Suruga Province in the [[Heian period]]. Also, the date of 901 is given for the foundation of the Sengen Jinja, as a subsidiary branch of the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], and initially was referred to as the "Shingu" (new shrine). Through the [[Kamakura period|Kamakura]] and [[Muromachi period]]s, the shrines enjoyed the patronage of the powerful warrior clans who dominated the Suruga area: the [[Minamoto clan]], [[Hōjō clan]], [[Imagawa clan]], [[Takeda clan]] and the [[Tokugawa clan]]. In particular, the first Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]'', [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], sponsored the rebuilding of the shrines after his retirement to nearby [[Sumpu Castle]], and subsequent Shogun continued to worship at the shrines throughout the [[Edo period]]. The 3rd ''shōgun'', [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] granted the shrines lands with 2313 [[koku]] in revenue for their upkeep. However, the shrine complex burned down in a fire of 1804. It was rebuilt over a 60-year period at a cost of over 100,000 gold ''[[ryō]]'' by the [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] in the its flamboyant [[Momoyama period|Momoyama style]], with extensive use of [[lacquer]], wood carvings, and [[gold leaf]]. Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]], forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. In the [[modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], Shizuoka Sengen was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Shōsha''|国幣小社}}. ==Subsidiary shrines== In addition to the three main shrines, the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja complex also has four subsidiary shrines: * Hayama Jinja (麓山神社) dedicated to [[Ohoyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and to [[Yamato-takeru]]. It was founded in 1878 * Yachiho Jinja (八千戈神社), an amalgamation of 18 small shrines and 13 small chapels found in the surrounding area. It was founded in 1873. * Sukunahiko Jinja (少彦名神社), formerly the [[Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi-do]] of Kambe Jinja, turned into a shrine in Meiji period due to [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Buddhism from Shinto]]. * Tamahoko Jinja (玉鉾神) dedicated to the four main Edo period ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholars. ==Cultural properties== Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]] (ICP), forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine's non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). It was founded in 1873. * Sukunahiko Jinja (少彦名神社), formerly the [[Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi-do]] of Kambe Jinja, turned into a shrine in Meiji period due to [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Buddhism from Shinto]]. * Tamahoko Jinja (玉鉾神) dedicated to the four main Edo period ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholars. ==Cultural properties== Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]] (ICP), forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine's non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} {{coord|35|07|19|N|138|55|08|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shizuoka, Shizuoka]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Shizuoka Sengen Jinja
静岡浅間神社 | image = Shizuoka Sengen Shrine Ohaiden.jpg | caption = Prayer Hall of Shizuoka Sengen Jinja | type = Asama shrine | dedication = [[Ookuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Konohanasakuya-hime]],
[[Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto]] | founded = | closed = | founder = | priest = | location_map = Japan | coordinates ={{coord|35|07|19|N|138|55|08|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | address = 102-1 Miyagasaki-chō, Aoi-ku, Shizuoka, 420-0868 | phone = 054-245-1820 | website = http://www.shizuokasengen.net/ }} {{nihongo|'''Shizuoka Sengen Jinja'''|静岡浅間神社}} is the name for a collective group of three [[Shinto shrine]]s now forming a single religious corporation, located at Mount Shizuhata in [[Aoi-ku, Shizuoka]], in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. These shrines are the Kanbe Jinja (神部神社), Sengen Jinja (浅間神社), and Ōtoshimioya Jinja (大歳御祖神社). The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 5. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Kambe Jinja is the [[Ookuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]], who is regarded as the mythical founding deity of [[Suruga Province]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Sengen Jinja is the [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the deity of [[Mount Fuji]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Ohtoshimioya Shrine is the [[Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto]], who appears in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as a daughter of [[Susano-o]], and a ''kami'' protecting markets and commerce. ==History== The date of the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja's foundation is unknown. The area has been inhabited since prehistoric times, and a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mound]] has been excavated at [[Shizuhatagayama Kofun|Mount Shizuhata]]. Per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', the area was colonized by the [[Hata clan]] during this period. According to unsubstantiated shrine legend, the foundation of the Kanbe Jinja dates to the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]], that of the Ōtoshimioya Shrine to the reign of [[Emperor Ōjin]], both from the Kofun period. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, Kanbe Jinja was given national recognition and status of the ''Sōja'' of Suruga Province in the [[Heian period]]. Also, the date of 901 is given for the foundation of the Sengen Jinja, as a subsidiary branch of the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], and initially was referred to as the "Shingu" (new shrine). Through the [[Kamakura period|Kamakura]] and [[Muromachi period]]s, the shrines enjoyed the patronage of the powerful warrior clans who dominated the Suruga area: the [[Minamoto clan]], [[Hōjō clan]], [[Imagawa clan]], [[Takeda clan]] and the [[Tokugawa clan]]. In particular, the first Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]'', [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], sponsored the rebuilding of the shrines after his retirement to nearby [[Sumpu Castle]], and subsequent ''shōguns'' continued to worship at the shrines throughout the [[Edo period]]. The 3rd ''shōgun'', [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]], granted the shrines lands with 2313 ''[[koku]]'' in revenue for their upkeep. However, the shrine complex burned down in a fire of 1804. It was rebuilt over a 60-year period at a cost of over 100,000 gold ''[[ryō]]'' by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the its flamboyant [[Momoyama period|Momoyama style]], with extensive use of [[lacquer]], wood carvings, and [[gold leaf]]. Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]], forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. In the [[modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], Shizuoka Sengen was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Shōsha''|国幣小社}}. ==Subsidiary shrines== In addition to the three main shrines, the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja complex also has four subsidiary shrines: * Hayama Jinja (麓山神社) dedicated to [[Ohoyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and to [[Yamato Takeru]]. It was founded in 1878 * Yachiho Jinja (八千戈神社), an amalgamation of 18 small shrines and 13 small chapels found in the surrounding area. It was founded in 1873. * Sukunahiko Jinja (少彦名神社), formerly the [[Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi-do]] of Kambe Jinja, turned into a shrine in Meiji period due to [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Buddhism from Shinto]]. * Tamahoko Jinja (玉鉾神) dedicated to the four main Edo period ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholars. ==Cultural properties== Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]] (ICP), forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine's non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shizuoka, Shizuoka]]

According to unsubstantiated shrine legend, the foundation of the Kanbe Jinja dates to the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]], that of the Ōtoshimioya Shrine to the reign of [[Emperor Ōjin]], both from the Kofun period. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, Kanbe Jinja was given national recognition and status of the ''Sōja'' of Suruga Province in the [[Heian period]]. Also, the date of 901 is given for the foundation of the Sengen Jinja, as a subsidiary branch of the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], and initially was referred to as the "Shingu" (new shrine). Through the [[Kamakura period|Kamakura]] and [[Muromachi period]]s, the shrines enjoyed the patronage of the powerful warrior clans who dominated the Suruga area: the [[Minamoto clan]], [[Hōjō clan]], [[Imagawa clan]], [[Takeda clan]] and the [[Tokugawa clan]]. In particular, the first Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]'', [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], sponsored the rebuilding of the shrines after his retirement to nearby [[Sumpu Castle]], and subsequent ''shōguns'' continued to worship at the shrines throughout the [[Edo period]]. The 3rd ''shōgun'', [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]], granted the shrines lands with 2313 ''[[koku]]'' in revenue for their upkeep. However, the shrine complex burned down in a fire of 1804. It was rebuilt over a 60-year period at a cost of over 100,000 gold ''[[ryō]]'' by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the its flamboyant [[Momoyama period|Momoyama style]], with extensive use of [[lacquer]], wood carvings, and [[gold leaf]]. Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]], forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. In the [[modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], Shizuoka Sengen was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Shōsha''|国幣小社}}. ==Subsidiary shrines== In addition to the three main shrines, the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja complex also has four subsidiary shrines: * Hayama Jinja (麓山神社) dedicated to [[Ohoyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and to [[Yamato Takeru]]. It was founded in 1878 * Yachiho Jinja (八千戈神社), an amalgamation of 18 small shrines and 13 small chapels found in the surrounding area. It was founded in 1873. * Sukunahiko Jinja (少彦名神社), formerly the [[Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi-do]] of Kambe Jinja, turned into a shrine in Meiji period due to [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Buddhism from Shinto]]. * Tamahoko Jinja (玉鉾神) dedicated to the four main Edo period ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholars. ==Cultural properties== Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]] (ICP), forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine's non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commonscat|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shizuoka, Shizuoka]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Shizuoka Sengen Jinja
静岡浅間神社 | image = Shizuoka Sengen Shrine Ohaiden.jpg | caption = Prayer Hall of Shizuoka Sengen Jinja | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|59|01|N|138|22|31|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Asama shrine | deity = [[Ookuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Konohanasakuya-hime]],
[[Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 102-1 Miyagasaki-chō, Aoi-ku, Shizuoka, 420-0868 | website = {{URL|http://www.shizuokasengen.net/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Shizuoka Sengen Jinja'''|静岡浅間神社}} is the name for a collective group of three [[Shinto shrine]]s now forming a single religious corporation, located at Mount Shizuhata in [[Aoi-ku, Shizuoka]], in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. These shrines are the Kanbe Jinja (神部神社), Sengen Jinja (浅間神社), and Ōtoshimioya Jinja (大歳御祖神社). The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 5. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Kambe Jinja is the [[Ookuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]], who is regarded as the mythical founding deity of [[Suruga Province]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Sengen Jinja is the [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the deity of [[Mount Fuji]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Ohtoshimioya Shrine is the [[Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto]], who appears in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as a daughter of [[Susano-o]], and a ''kami'' protecting markets and commerce. ==History== The date of the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja's foundation is unknown. The area has been inhabited since prehistoric times, and a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mound]] has been excavated at [[Shizuhatagayama Kofun|Mount Shizuhata]]. Per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', the area was colonized by the [[Hata clan]] during this period. According to unsubstantiated shrine legend, the foundation of the Kanbe Jinja dates to the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]], that of the Ōtoshimioya Shrine to the reign of [[Emperor Ōjin]], both from the Kofun period. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, Kanbe Jinja was given national recognition and status of the ''Sōja'' of Suruga Province in the [[Heian period]]. Also, the date of 901 is given for the foundation of the Sengen Jinja, as a subsidiary branch of the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], and initially was referred to as the "Shingu" (new shrine). Through the [[Kamakura period|Kamakura]] and [[Muromachi period]]s, the shrines enjoyed the patronage of the powerful warrior clans who dominated the Suruga area: the [[Minamoto clan]], [[Hōjō clan]], [[Imagawa clan]], [[Takeda clan]] and the [[Tokugawa clan]]. In particular, the first Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]'', [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], sponsored the rebuilding of the shrines after his retirement to nearby [[Sumpu Castle]], and subsequent ''shōguns'' continued to worship at the shrines throughout the [[Edo period]]. The 3rd ''shōgun'', [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]], granted the shrines lands with 2313 ''[[koku]]'' in revenue for their upkeep. However, the shrine complex burned down in a fire of 1804. It was rebuilt over a 60-year period at a cost of over 100,000 gold ''[[ryō]]'' by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in its flamboyant [[Momoyama period|Momoyama style]], with extensive use of [[lacquer]], wood carvings, and [[gold leaf]]. Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]], forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. In the [[modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], Shizuoka Sengen was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Shōsha''|国幣小社}}. ==Subsidiary shrines== In addition to the three main shrines, the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja complex also has four subsidiary shrines: * Hayama Jinja (麓山神社) dedicated to [[Ohoyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and to [[Yamato Takeru]]. It was founded in 1878 * Yachiho Jinja (八千戈神社), an amalgamation of 18 small shrines and 13 small chapels found in the surrounding area. It was founded in 1873. * Sukunahiko Jinja (少彦名神社), formerly the [[Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi-do]] of Kambe Jinja, turned into a shrine in Meiji period due to [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Buddhism from Shinto]]. * Tamahoko Jinja (玉鉾神) dedicated to the four main Edo period ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholars. ==Cultural properties== Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]] (ICP), forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine's non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan}} * [[List of Shinto shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shizuoka (city)]]

It was rebuilt over a 60-year period at a cost of over 100,000 gold ''[[ryō]]'' by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in its flamboyant [[Momoyama period|Momoyama style]], with extensive use of [[lacquer]], wood carvings, and [[gold leaf]]. Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]], forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. In the [[modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], Shizuoka Sengen was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Shōsha''|国幣小社}}. ==Subsidiary shrines== In addition to the three main shrines, the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja complex also has four subsidiary shrines: * Hayama Jinja (麓山神社) dedicated to [[Ohoyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and to [[Yamato Takeru]]. It was founded in 1878 * Yachiho Jinja (八千戈神社), an amalgamation of 18 small shrines and 13 small chapels found in the surrounding area. It was founded in 1873. * Sukunahiko Jinja (少彦名神社), formerly the [[Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi-do]] of Kambe Jinja, turned into a shrine in Meiji period due to [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Buddhism from Shinto]]. * Tamahoko Jinja (玉鉾神) dedicated to the four main Edo period ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholars. ==Cultural properties== Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]] (ICP), forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine's non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan}} * [[List of Shinto shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shizuoka (city)]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Shizuoka Sengen Jinja
静岡浅間神社 | image = Shizuoka Sengen Shrine Ohaiden.jpg | caption = Prayer Hall of Shizuoka Sengen Jinja | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|59|01|N|138|22|31|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Asama shrine | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Konohanasakuya-hime]],
[[Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 102-1 Miyagasaki-chō, Aoi-ku, Shizuoka, 420-0868 | website = {{URL|http://www.shizuokasengen.net/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Shizuoka Sengen Jinja'''|静岡浅間神社}} is the name for a collective group of three [[Shinto shrine]]s now forming a single religious corporation, located at Mount Shizuhata in [[Aoi-ku, Shizuoka]], in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. These shrines are the Kanbe Jinja (神部神社), Sengen Jinja (浅間神社), and Ōtoshimioya Jinja (大歳御祖神社). The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 5. [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Kambe Jinja is the [[Ookuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]], who is regarded as the mythical founding deity of [[Suruga Province]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Sengen Jinja is the [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the deity of [[Mount Fuji]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Ohtoshimioya Shrine is the [[Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto]], who appears in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as a daughter of [[Susano-o]], and a ''kami'' protecting markets and commerce. ==History== The date of the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja's foundation is unknown. The area has been inhabited since prehistoric times, and a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mound]] has been excavated at [[Shizuhatagayama Kofun|Mount Shizuhata]]. Per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', the area was colonized by the [[Hata clan]] during this period. According to unsubstantiated shrine legend, the foundation of the Kanbe Jinja dates to the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]], that of the Ōtoshimioya Shrine to the reign of [[Emperor Ōjin]], both from the Kofun period. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, Kanbe Jinja was given national recognition and status of the ''Sōja'' of Suruga Province in the [[Heian period]]. Also, the date of 901 is given for the foundation of the Sengen Jinja, as a subsidiary branch of the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], and initially was referred to as the "Shingu" (new shrine). Through the [[Kamakura period|Kamakura]] and [[Muromachi period]]s, the shrines enjoyed the patronage of the powerful warrior clans who dominated the Suruga area: the [[Minamoto clan]], [[Hōjō clan]], [[Imagawa clan]], [[Takeda clan]] and the [[Tokugawa clan]]. In particular, the first Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]'', [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], sponsored the rebuilding of the shrines after his retirement to nearby [[Sumpu Castle]], and subsequent ''shōguns'' continued to worship at the shrines throughout the [[Edo period]]. The 3rd ''shōgun'', [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]], granted the shrines lands with 2313 ''[[koku]]'' in revenue for their upkeep. However, the shrine complex burned down in a fire of 1804. It was rebuilt over a 60-year period at a cost of over 100,000 gold ''[[ryō]]'' by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in its flamboyant [[Momoyama period|Momoyama style]], with extensive use of [[lacquer]], wood carvings, and [[gold leaf]]. Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]], forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. In the [[modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], Shizuoka Sengen was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Shōsha''|国幣小社}}. ==Subsidiary shrines== In addition to the three main shrines, the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja complex also has four subsidiary shrines: * Hayama Jinja (麓山神社) dedicated to [[Ohoyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and to [[Yamato Takeru]]. It was founded in 1878 * Yachiho Jinja (八千戈神社), an amalgamation of 18 small shrines and 13 small chapels found in the surrounding area. It was founded in 1873. * Sukunahiko Jinja (少彦名神社), formerly the [[Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi-do]] of Kambe Jinja, turned into a shrine in Meiji period due to [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Buddhism from Shinto]]. * Tamahoko Jinja (玉鉾神) dedicated to the four main Edo period ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholars. ==Cultural properties== Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]] (ICP), forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine's non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan}} * [[List of Shinto shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shizuoka (city)]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Sōja]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Shizuoka Sengen Jinja
静岡浅間神社 | image = Shizuoka Sengen Shrine Ohaiden.jpg | caption = Prayer Hall of Shizuoka Sengen Jinja | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|59|01|N|138|22|31|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Asama shrine | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Konohanasakuya-hime]],
[[Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 102-1 Miyagasaki-chō, Aoi-ku, Shizuoka, 420-0868 | website = {{URL|http://www.shizuokasengen.net/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Shizuoka Sengen Jinja'''|静岡浅間神社}} is the name for a collective group of three [[Shinto shrine]]s now forming a single religious corporation, located at Mount Shizuhata in [[Aoi-ku, Shizuoka]], in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. These shrines are the Kanbe Jinja (神部神社), Sengen Jinja (浅間神社), and Ōtoshimioya Jinja (大歳御祖神社). The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 5. It is the [[Sōja shrine]] of [[Suruga Province]]. [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Kambe Jinja is the [[Ookuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]], who is regarded as the mythical founding deity of [[Suruga Province]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Sengen Jinja is the [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the deity of [[Mount Fuji]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Ohtoshimioya Shrine is the [[Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto]], who appears in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as a daughter of [[Susano-o]], and a ''kami'' protecting markets and commerce. ==History== The date of the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja's foundation is unknown. The area has been inhabited since prehistoric times, and a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mound]] has been excavated at [[Shizuhatagayama Kofun|Mount Shizuhata]]. Per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', the area was colonized by the [[Hata clan]] during this period. According to unsubstantiated shrine legend, the foundation of the Kanbe Jinja dates to the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]], that of the Ōtoshimioya Shrine to the reign of [[Emperor Ōjin]], both from the Kofun period. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, Kanbe Jinja was given national recognition and status of the ''Sōja'' of Suruga Province in the [[Heian period]]. Also, the date of 901 is given for the foundation of the Sengen Jinja, as a subsidiary branch of the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], and initially was referred to as the "Shingu" (new shrine). Through the [[Kamakura period|Kamakura]] and [[Muromachi period]]s, the shrines enjoyed the patronage of the powerful warrior clans who dominated the Suruga area: the [[Minamoto clan]], [[Hōjō clan]], [[Imagawa clan]], [[Takeda clan]] and the [[Tokugawa clan]]. In particular, the first Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]'', [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], sponsored the rebuilding of the shrines after his retirement to nearby [[Sumpu Castle]], and subsequent ''shōguns'' continued to worship at the shrines throughout the [[Edo period]]. The 3rd ''shōgun'', [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]], granted the shrines lands with 2313 ''[[koku]]'' in revenue for their upkeep. However, the shrine complex burned down in a fire of 1804. It was rebuilt over a 60-year period at a cost of over 100,000 gold ''[[ryō]]'' by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in its flamboyant [[Momoyama period|Momoyama style]], with extensive use of [[lacquer]], wood carvings, and [[gold leaf]]. Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]], forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. In the [[modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], Shizuoka Sengen was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}. ==Subsidiary shrines== In addition to the three main shrines, the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja complex also has four subsidiary shrines: * Hayama Jinja (麓山神社) dedicated to [[Ohoyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and to [[Yamato Takeru]]. It was founded in 1878 * Yachiho Jinja (八千戈神社), an amalgamation of 18 small shrines and 13 small chapels found in the surrounding area. It was founded in 1873. * Sukunahiko Jinja (少彦名神社), formerly the [[Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi-do]] of Kambe Jinja, turned into a shrine in Meiji period due to [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Buddhism from Shinto]]. * Tamahoko Jinja (玉鉾神) dedicated to the four main Edo period ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholars. ==Cultural properties== Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]] (ICP), forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine's non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan}} * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]] ([[Suruga Province]] [[Ichinomiya]]) ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shizuoka (city)]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]]

The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine's non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan}} * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]] ([[Suruga Province]] [[Ichinomiya]]) ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shizuoka (city)]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Asama shrines]]

revision.comment: Removing link(s) to "Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto": not notable enough for a Japanese wikipedia article.

The area has been inhabited since prehistoric times, and a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mound]] has been excavated at [[Shizuhatagayama Kofun|Mount Shizuhata]]. According to unsubstantiated shrine legend, the foundation of the Kanbe Jinja dates to the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]], that of the Ōtoshimioya Shrine to the reign of [[Emperor Ōjin]], both from the Kofun period. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, Kanbe Jinja was given national recognition and status of the ''Sōja'' of Suruga Province in the [[Heian period]]. Also, the date of 901 is given for the foundation of the Sengen Jinja, as a subsidiary branch of the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], and initially was referred to as the "Shingu" (new shrine). Through the [[Kamakura period|Kamakura]] and [[Muromachi period]]s, the shrines enjoyed the patronage of the powerful warrior clans who dominated the Suruga area: the [[Minamoto clan]], [[Hōjō clan]], [[Imagawa clan]], [[Takeda clan]] and the [[Tokugawa clan]]. In particular, the first Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]'', [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], sponsored the rebuilding of the shrines after his retirement to nearby [[Sumpu Castle]], and subsequent ''shōguns'' continued to worship at the shrines throughout the [[Edo period]]. The 3rd ''shōgun'', [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]], granted the shrines lands with 2313 ''[[koku]]'' in revenue for their upkeep. However, the shrine complex burned down in a fire of 1804. It was rebuilt over a 60-year period at a cost of over 100,000 gold ''[[ryō]]'' by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in its flamboyant [[Momoyama period|Momoyama style]], with extensive use of [[lacquer]], wood carvings, and [[gold leaf]]. Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]], forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. In the [[modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], Shizuoka Sengen was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}. ==Subsidiary shrines== In addition to the three main shrines, the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja complex also has four subsidiary shrines: * Hayama Jinja (麓山神社) dedicated to [[Ohoyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and to [[Yamato Takeru]]. It was founded in 1878 * Yachiho Jinja (八千戈神社), an amalgamation of 18 small shrines and 13 small chapels found in the surrounding area. It was founded in 1873. * Sukunahiko Jinja (少彦名神社), formerly the [[Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi-do]] of Kambe Jinja, turned into a shrine in Meiji period due to [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Buddhism from Shinto]]. * Tamahoko Jinja (玉鉾神) dedicated to the four main Edo period ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholars. ==Cultural properties== Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]] (ICP), forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine's non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan}} * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]] ([[Suruga Province]] [[Ichinomiya]]) ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shizuoka (city)]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Asama shrines]]

{{ill|Kamuo Ichihime|ja|神大市比売|lt=Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 102-1 Miyagasaki-chō, Aoi-ku, Shizuoka, 420-0868 | website = {{URL|http://www.shizuokasengen.net/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Shizuoka Sengen Jinja'''|静岡浅間神社}} is the name for a collective group of three [[Shinto shrine]]s now forming a single religious corporation, located at Mount Shizuhata in [[Aoi-ku, Shizuoka]], in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Sengen Jinja is the [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the deity of [[Mount Fuji]]. It was rebuilt over a 60-year period at a cost of over 100,000 gold ''[[ryō]]'' by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in its flamboyant [[Momoyama period|Momoyama style]], with extensive use of [[lacquer]], wood carvings, and [[gold leaf]]. It was founded in 1878 * Yachiho Jinja (八千戈神社), an amalgamation of 18 small shrines and 13 small chapels found in the surrounding area. It was founded in 1873. * Sukunahiko Jinja (少彦名神社), formerly the [[Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi-do]] of Kambe Jinja, turned into a shrine in Meiji period due to [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Buddhism from Shinto]]. * Tamahoko Jinja (玉鉾神) dedicated to the four main Edo period ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholars. ==Cultural properties== Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]] (ICP), forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine's non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan}} * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]] ([[Suruga Province]] [[Ichinomiya]]) ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shizuoka (city)]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Asama shrines]] {{Asama Faith}}

{{Infobox religious building | name = Shizuoka Sengen Jinja
静岡浅間神社 | image = Shizuoka Sengen Shrine Ohaiden.jpg | caption = Prayer Hall of Shizuoka Sengen Jinja | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|59|01|N|138|22|31|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Asama shrine | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Konohanasakuya-hime]],
[[Kamuo Ichihime|Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 102-1 Miyagasaki-chō, Aoi-ku, Shizuoka, 420-0868 | website = {{URL|http://www.shizuokasengen.net/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Shizuoka Sengen Jinja'''|静岡浅間神社}} is the name for a collective group of three [[Shinto shrine]]s now forming a single religious corporation, located at Mount Shizuhata in [[Aoi-ku, Shizuoka]], in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. These shrines are the Kanbe Jinja (神部神社), Sengen Jinja (浅間神社), and Ōtoshimioya Jinja (大歳御祖神社). The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 5. It is the [[Sōja shrine]] of [[Suruga Province]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Kambe Jinja is the [[Ookuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]], who is regarded as the mythical founding deity of [[Suruga Province]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Sengen Jinja is the [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the deity of [[Mount Fuji]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Ohtoshimioya Shrine is the Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto (大歳御祖命), who appears in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as a wife of [[Susano-o]], and a ''kami'' protecting markets and commerce. ==History== The date of the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja's foundation is unknown. The area has been inhabited since prehistoric times, and a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mound]] has been excavated at [[Shizuhatagayama Kofun|Mount Shizuhata]]. Per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', the area was colonized by the [[Hata clan]] during this period. According to unsubstantiated shrine legend, the foundation of the Kanbe Jinja dates to the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]], that of the Ōtoshimioya Shrine to the reign of [[Emperor Ōjin]], both from the Kofun period. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, Kanbe Jinja was given national recognition and status of the ''Sōja'' of Suruga Province in the [[Heian period]]. Also, the date of 901 is given for the foundation of the Sengen Jinja, as a subsidiary branch of the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], and initially was referred to as the "Shingu" (new shrine). Through the [[Kamakura period|Kamakura]] and [[Muromachi period]]s, the shrines enjoyed the patronage of the powerful warrior clans who dominated the Suruga area: the [[Minamoto clan]], [[Hōjō clan]], [[Imagawa clan]], [[Takeda clan]] and the [[Tokugawa clan]]. In particular, the first Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]'', [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], sponsored the rebuilding of the shrines after his retirement to nearby [[Sumpu Castle]], and subsequent ''shōguns'' continued to worship at the shrines throughout the [[Edo period]]. The 3rd ''shōgun'', [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]], granted the shrines lands with 2313 ''[[koku]]'' in revenue for their upkeep. However, the shrine complex burned down in a fire of 1804. It was rebuilt over a 60-year period at a cost of over 100,000 gold ''[[ryō]]'' by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in its flamboyant [[Momoyama period|Momoyama style]], with extensive use of [[lacquer]], wood carvings, and [[gold leaf]]. Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]], forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. In the [[modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], Shizuoka Sengen was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}. ==Subsidiary shrines== In addition to the three main shrines, the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja complex also has four subsidiary shrines: * Hayama Jinja (麓山神社) dedicated to [[Ohoyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and to [[Yamato Takeru]]. It was founded in 1878 * Yachiho Jinja (八千戈神社), an amalgamation of 18 small shrines and 13 small chapels found in the surrounding area. It was founded in 1873. * Sukunahiko Jinja (少彦名神社), formerly the [[Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi-do]] of Kambe Jinja, turned into a shrine in Meiji period due to [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Buddhism from Shinto]]. * Tamahoko Jinja (玉鉾神) dedicated to the four main Edo period ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholars. ==Cultural properties== Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]] (ICP), forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine's non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan}} * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]] ([[Suruga Province]] [[Ichinomiya]]) ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shizuoka (city)]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Asama shrines]] {{Asama Faith}}

Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shizuoka (city)]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]][[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Asama shrines]] {{Asama Faith}}

{{Infobox religious building | name = Shizuoka Sengen Jinja
静岡浅間神社 | image = Shizuoka Sengen Shrine Ohaiden.jpg | caption = Prayer Hall of Shizuoka Sengen Jinja | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|59|01|N|138|22|31|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Asama shrine | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Konohanasakuya-hime]],
[[Kamuo Ichihime|Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 102-1 Miyagasaki-chō, Aoi-ku, Shizuoka, 420-0868 | website = {{URL|http://www.shizuokasengen.net/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Shizuoka Sengen Jinja'''|静岡浅間神社}} is the name for a collective group of three [[Shinto shrine]]s now forming a single religious corporation, located at Mount Shizuhata in [[Aoi-ku, Shizuoka]], in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. These shrines are the Kanbe Jinja (神部神社), Sengen Jinja (浅間神社), and Ōtoshimioya Jinja (大歳御祖神社). The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 5. It is the [[Sōja shrine]] of [[Suruga Province]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Kambe Jinja is the [[Ookuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]], who is regarded as the mythical founding deity of [[Suruga Province]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Sengen Jinja is the [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the deity of [[Mount Fuji]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Ohtoshimioya Shrine is the Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto (大歳御祖命), who appears in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as a wife of [[Susano-o]], and a ''kami'' protecting markets and commerce. ==History== The date of the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja's foundation is unknown. The area has been inhabited since prehistoric times, and a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mound]] has been excavated at [[Shizuhatagayama Kofun|Mount Shizuhata]]. Per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', the area was colonized by the [[Hata clan]] during this period. According to unsubstantiated shrine legend, the foundation of the Kanbe Jinja dates to the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]], that of the Ōtoshimioya Shrine to the reign of [[Emperor Ōjin]], both from the Kofun period. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, Kanbe Jinja was given national recognition and status of the ''Sōja'' of Suruga Province in the [[Heian period]]. Also, the date of 901 is given for the foundation of the Sengen Jinja, as a subsidiary branch of the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], and initially was referred to as the "Shingu" (new shrine). Through the [[Kamakura period|Kamakura]] and [[Muromachi period]]s, the shrines enjoyed the patronage of the powerful warrior clans who dominated the Suruga area: the [[Minamoto clan]], [[Hōjō clan]], [[Imagawa clan]], [[Takeda clan]] and the [[Tokugawa clan]]. In particular, the first Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]'', [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], sponsored the rebuilding of the shrines after his retirement to nearby [[Sumpu Castle]], and subsequent ''shōguns'' continued to worship at the shrines throughout the [[Edo period]]. The 3rd ''shōgun'', [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]], granted the shrines lands with 2313 ''[[koku]]'' in revenue for their upkeep. However, the shrine complex burned down in a fire of 1804. It was rebuilt over a 60-year period at a cost of over 100,000 gold ''[[ryō]]'' by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in its flamboyant [[Momoyama period|Momoyama style]], with extensive use of [[lacquer]], wood carvings, and [[gold leaf]]. Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]], forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. In the [[modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], Shizuoka Sengen was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}. ==Subsidiary shrines== In addition to the three main shrines, the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja complex also has four subsidiary shrines: * Hayama Jinja (麓山神社) dedicated to [[Ohoyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and to [[Yamato Takeru]]. It was founded in 1878 * Yachiho Jinja (八千戈神社), an amalgamation of 18 small shrines and 13 small chapels found in the surrounding area. It was founded in 1873. * Sukunahiko Jinja (少彦名神社), formerly the [[Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi-do]] of Kambe Jinja, turned into a shrine in Meiji period due to [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Buddhism from Shinto]]. * Tamahoko Jinja (玉鉾神) dedicated to the four main Edo period ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholars. ==Cultural properties== Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]] (ICP), forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine's non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan}} * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]] ([[Suruga Province]] [[Ichinomiya]]) ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shizuoka (city)]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]][[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:pages from wikipedia]][[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]][[Category:pages from wikipedia]] {{Asama Faith}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Shizuoka Sengen Jinja
静岡浅間神社 | image = Shizuoka Sengen Shrine Ohaiden.jpg | caption = Prayer Hall of Shizuoka Sengen Jinja | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|59|01|N|138|22|31|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Asama shrine | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Konohanasakuya-hime]],
[[Kamuo Ichihime|Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 102-1 Miyagasaki-chō, Aoi-ku, Shizuoka, 420-0868 | website = {{URL|http://www.shizuokasengen.net/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Shizuoka Sengen Jinja'''|静岡浅間神社}} is the name for a collective group of three [[Shinto shrine]]s now forming a single religious corporation, located at Mount Shizuhata in [[Aoi-ku, Shizuoka]], in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. These shrines are the Kanbe Jinja (神部神社), Sengen Jinja (浅間神社), and Ōtoshimioya Jinja (大歳御祖神社). The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 5. It is the [[Sōja shrine]] of [[Suruga Province]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Kambe Jinja is the [[Ookuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]], who is regarded as the mythical founding deity of [[Suruga Province]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Sengen Jinja is the [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the deity of [[Mount Fuji]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Ohtoshimioya Shrine is the Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto (大歳御祖命), who appears in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as a wife of [[Susano-o]], and a ''kami'' protecting markets and commerce. ==History== The date of the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja's foundation is unknown. The area has been inhabited since prehistoric times, and a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mound]] has been excavated at [[Shizuhatagayama Kofun|Mount Shizuhata]]. Per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', the area was colonized by the [[Hata clan]] during this period. According to unsubstantiated shrine legend, the foundation of the Kanbe Jinja dates to the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]], that of the Ōtoshimioya Shrine to the reign of [[Emperor Ōjin]], both from the Kofun period. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, Kanbe Jinja was given national recognition and status of the ''Sōja'' of Suruga Province in the [[Heian period]]. Also, the date of 901 is given for the foundation of the Sengen Jinja, as a subsidiary branch of the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], and initially was referred to as the "Shingu" (new shrine). Through the [[Kamakura period|Kamakura]] and [[Muromachi period]]s, the shrines enjoyed the patronage of the powerful warrior clans who dominated the Suruga area: the [[Minamoto clan]], [[Hōjō clan]], [[Imagawa clan]], [[Takeda clan]] and the [[Tokugawa clan]]. In particular, the first Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]'', [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], sponsored the rebuilding of the shrines after his retirement to nearby [[Sumpu Castle]], and subsequent ''shōguns'' continued to worship at the shrines throughout the [[Edo period]]. The 3rd ''shōgun'', [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]], granted the shrines lands with 2313 ''[[koku]]'' in revenue for their upkeep. However, the shrine complex burned down in a fire of 1804. It was rebuilt over a 60-year period at a cost of over 100,000 gold ''[[ryō]]'' by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in its flamboyant [[Momoyama period|Momoyama style]], with extensive use of [[lacquer]], wood carvings, and [[gold leaf]]. Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]], forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. In the [[modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], Shizuoka Sengen was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}. ==Subsidiary shrines== In addition to the three main shrines, the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja complex also has four subsidiary shrines: * Hayama Jinja (麓山神社) dedicated to [[Ohoyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and to [[Yamato Takeru]]. It was founded in 1878 * Yachiho Jinja (八千戈神社), an amalgamation of 18 small shrines and 13 small chapels found in the surrounding area. It was founded in 1873. * Sukunahiko Jinja (少彦名神社), formerly the [[Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi-do]] of Kambe Jinja, turned into a shrine in Meiji period due to [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Buddhism from Shinto]]. * Tamahoko Jinja (玉鉾神) dedicated to the four main Edo period ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholars. ==Cultural properties== Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]] (ICP), forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine's non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan}} * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]] ([[Suruga Province]] [[Ichinomiya]]) ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shizuoka (city)]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]][[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Improperly tagged templates and modules2]] [[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]][[Category:Improperly tagged templates and modules2]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Articles tagged as templates and modules]] {{Asama Faith}}
