[[arz:معبد ضريح شيزوكا سينجين]][[sn:commons:Category:Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja]][[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]][[es:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[eu:Shizuoka Sengen santutegia]][[fr:Shizuoka Sengen-jinja]][[it:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[ja:静岡浅間神社]][[zh:靜岡淺間神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q500368]] Wikidata name: Shizuoka Sengen Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Shizuoka Sengen Jinja
静岡浅間神社 | image = Shizuoka Sengen Shrine Ohaiden.jpg | caption = Prayer Hall of Shizuoka Sengen Jinja | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|59|01|N|138|22|31|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Asama shrine | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Konohanasakuya-hime]],
[[Kamuo Ichihime|Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 102-1 Miyagasaki-chō, Aoi-ku, Shizuoka, 420-0868 | website = {{URL|http://www.shizuokasengen.net/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Shizuoka Sengen Jinja'''|静岡浅間神社}} is the name for a collective group of three [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s now forming a single religious corporation, located at Mount Shizuhata in [[:en:Aoi-ku, Shizuoka|Aoi-ku, Shizuoka]], in [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. These shrines are the Kanbe Jinja (神部神社), Sengen Jinja (浅間神社), and Ōtoshimioya Jinja (大歳御祖神社). The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 5. It is the [[Sōja shrine]] of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Kambe Jinja is the [[:en:Ookuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]], who is regarded as the mythical founding deity of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Sengen Jinja is the [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the deity of [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Ohtoshimioya Shrine is the Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto (大歳御祖命), who appears in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as a wife of [[:en:Susano-o|Susano-o]], and a ''kami'' protecting markets and commerce. ==History== The date of the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja's foundation is unknown. The area has been inhabited since prehistoric times, and a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]] has been excavated at [[Shizuhatagayama Kofun|Mount Shizuhata]]. Per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', the area was colonized by the [[:en:Hata clan|Hata clan]] during this period. According to unsubstantiated shrine legend, the foundation of the Kanbe Jinja dates to the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], that of the Ōtoshimioya Shrine to the reign of [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]], both from the Kofun period. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, Kanbe Jinja was given national recognition and status of the ''Sōja'' of Suruga Province in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. Also, the date of 901 is given for the foundation of the Sengen Jinja, as a subsidiary branch of the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], and initially was referred to as the "Shingu" (new shrine). Through the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura]] and [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]s, the shrines enjoyed the patronage of the powerful warrior clans who dominated the Suruga area: the [[Minamoto clan]], [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], [[Imagawa clan]], [[Takeda clan]] and the [[Tokugawa clan]]. In particular, the first Tokugawa ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'', [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]], sponsored the rebuilding of the shrines after his retirement to nearby [[:en:Sumpu Castle|Sumpu Castle]], and subsequent ''shōguns'' continued to worship at the shrines throughout the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. The 3rd ''shōgun'', [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]], granted the shrines lands with 2313 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'' in revenue for their upkeep. However, the shrine complex burned down in a fire of 1804. It was rebuilt over a 60-year period at a cost of over 100,000 gold ''[[:en:Ryō|Ryō]]'' by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in its flamboyant [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Momoyama style]], with extensive use of [[:en:Lacquer|Lacquer]], wood carvings, and [[:en:Gold leaf|Gold leaf]]. Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]], forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Shizuoka Sengen was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}. ==Subsidiary shrines== In addition to the three main shrines, the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja complex also has four subsidiary shrines: * Hayama Jinja (麓山神社) dedicated to [[Ōyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and to [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]]. It was founded in 1878 * Yachiho Jinja (八千戈神社), an amalgamation of 18 small shrines and 13 small chapels found in the surrounding area. It was founded in 1873. * Sukunahiko Jinja (少彦名神社), formerly the [[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi-do]] of Kambe Jinja, turned into a shrine in Meiji period due to [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Buddhism from Shinto]]. * Tamahoko Jinja (玉鉾神) dedicated to the four main Edo period ''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]]'' scholars. ==Cultural properties== Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]] (ICP), forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine's non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan}} * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]] ([[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]] [[Ichinomiya]]) ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons Category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} {{Asama Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Sōja shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shizuoka (city)]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Asama shrines]] }} [[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[arz:معبد ضريح شيزوكا سينجين]] {{Commons category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} [[es:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]] [[eu:Shizuoka Sengen santutegia]] [[fr:Shizuoka Sengen-jinja]] [[it:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]] [[ja:静岡浅間神社]] [[zh:靜岡淺間神社]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q500368}}

The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine's non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan}} * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]] ([[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]] [[Ichinomiya]]) ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons Category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. revision.text:
[[arz:معبد ضريح شيزوكا سينجين]][[commons:Category:Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja]][[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]][[es:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[eu:Shizuoka Sengen santutegia]][[fr:Shizuoka Sengen-jinja]][[it:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[ja:静岡浅間神社]][[zh:靜岡淺間神社]] [[arz:معبد ضريح شيزوكا سينجين]][[commons:Category:Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja]][[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]][[es:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[eu:Shizuoka Sengen santutegia]][[fr:Shizuoka Sengen-jinja]][[it:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[ja:静岡浅間神社]][[zh:靜岡淺間神社]] [[arz:معبد ضريح شيزوكا سينجين]][[sn:commons:Category:Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja]][[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]][[es:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[eu:Shizuoka Sengen santutegia]][[fr:Shizuoka Sengen-jinja]][[it:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[ja:静岡浅間神社]][[zh:靜岡淺間神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q500368]] Wikidata name: Shizuoka Sengen Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Shizuoka Sengen Jinja
静岡浅間神社 | image = Shizuoka Sengen Shrine Ohaiden.jpg | caption = Prayer Hall of Shizuoka Sengen Jinja | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|59|01|N|138|22|31|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Asama shrine | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Konohanasakuya-hime]],
[[Kamuo Ichihime|Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 102-1 Miyagasaki-chō, Aoi-ku, Shizuoka, 420-0868 | website = {{URL|http://www.shizuokasengen.net/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Shizuoka Sengen Jinja'''|静岡浅間神社}} is the name for a collective group of three [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s now forming a single religious corporation, located at Mount Shizuhata in [[:en:Aoi-ku, Shizuoka|Aoi-ku, Shizuoka]], in [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. These shrines are the Kanbe Jinja (神部神社), Sengen Jinja (浅間神社), and Ōtoshimioya Jinja (大歳御祖神社). The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 5. It is the [[Sōja shrine]] of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Kambe Jinja is the [[:en:Ookuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]], who is regarded as the mythical founding deity of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Sengen Jinja is the [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the deity of [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Ohtoshimioya Shrine is the Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto (大歳御祖命), who appears in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as a wife of [[:en:Susano-o|Susano-o]], and a ''kami'' protecting markets and commerce. ==History== The date of the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja's foundation is unknown. The area has been inhabited since prehistoric times, and a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]] has been excavated at [[Shizuhatagayama Kofun|Mount Shizuhata]]. Per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', the area was colonized by the [[:en:Hata clan|Hata clan]] during this period. According to unsubstantiated shrine legend, the foundation of the Kanbe Jinja dates to the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], that of the Ōtoshimioya Shrine to the reign of [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]], both from the Kofun period. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, Kanbe Jinja was given national recognition and status of the ''Sōja'' of Suruga Province in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. Also, the date of 901 is given for the foundation of the Sengen Jinja, as a subsidiary branch of the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], and initially was referred to as the "Shingu" (new shrine). Through the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura]] and [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]s, the shrines enjoyed the patronage of the powerful warrior clans who dominated the Suruga area: the [[Minamoto clan]], [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], [[Imagawa clan]], [[Takeda clan]] and the [[Tokugawa clan]]. In particular, the first Tokugawa ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'', [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]], sponsored the rebuilding of the shrines after his retirement to nearby [[:en:Sumpu Castle|Sumpu Castle]], and subsequent ''shōguns'' continued to worship at the shrines throughout the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. The 3rd ''shōgun'', [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]], granted the shrines lands with 2313 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'' in revenue for their upkeep. However, the shrine complex burned down in a fire of 1804. It was rebuilt over a 60-year period at a cost of over 100,000 gold ''[[:en:Ryō|Ryō]]'' by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in its flamboyant [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Momoyama style]], with extensive use of [[:en:Lacquer|Lacquer]], wood carvings, and [[:en:Gold leaf|Gold leaf]]. Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]], forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Shizuoka Sengen was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}. ==Subsidiary shrines== In addition to the three main shrines, the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja complex also has four subsidiary shrines: * Hayama Jinja (麓山神社) dedicated to [[Ōyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and to [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]]. It was founded in 1878 * Yachiho Jinja (八千戈神社), an amalgamation of 18 small shrines and 13 small chapels found in the surrounding area. It was founded in 1873. * Sukunahiko Jinja (少彦名神社), formerly the [[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi-do]] of Kambe Jinja, turned into a shrine in Meiji period due to [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Buddhism from Shinto]]. * Tamahoko Jinja (玉鉾神) dedicated to the four main Edo period ''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]]'' scholars. ==Cultural properties== Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]] (ICP), forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine's non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan}} * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]] ([[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]] [[Ichinomiya]]) ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons Category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} {{Asama Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Sōja shrines}} {{draft categories| }} [[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] [[arz:معبد ضريح شيزوكا سينجين]] {{Commons category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} [[es:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]] [[eu:Shizuoka Sengen santutegia]] [[fr:Shizuoka Sengen-jinja]] [[it:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]] [[ja:静岡浅間神社]] [[zh:靜岡淺間神社]] {{wikidata link|Q500368}} [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shizuoka (city)]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

The 3rd ''shōgun'', [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]], granted the shrines lands with 2313 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'' in revenue for their upkeep. However, the shrine complex burned down in a fire of 1804. It was rebuilt over a 60-year period at a cost of over 100,000 gold ''[[:en:Ryō|Ryō]]'' by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in its flamboyant [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Momoyama style]], with extensive use of [[:en:Lacquer|Lacquer]], wood carvings, and [[:en:Gold leaf|Gold leaf]]. Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]], forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Shizuoka Sengen was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}. ==Subsidiary shrines== In addition to the three main shrines, the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja complex also has four subsidiary shrines: * Hayama Jinja (麓山神社) dedicated to [[Ōyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and to [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]]. It was founded in 1878 * Yachiho Jinja (八千戈神社), an amalgamation of 18 small shrines and 13 small chapels found in the surrounding area. It was founded in 1873. * Sukunahiko Jinja (少彦名神社), formerly the [[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi-do]] of Kambe Jinja, turned into a shrine in Meiji period due to [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Buddhism from Shinto]]. * Tamahoko Jinja (玉鉾神) dedicated to the four main Edo period ''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]]'' scholars. ==Cultural properties== Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]] (ICP), forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine's non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan}} * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]] ([[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]] [[Ichinomiya]]) ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons Category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). [[arz:معبد ضريح شيزوكا سينجين]][[commons:Category:Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja]][[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]][[es:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[eu:Shizuoka Sengen santutegia]][[fr:Shizuoka Sengen-jinja]][[it:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[ja:静岡浅間神社]][[zh:靜岡淺間神社]] [[arz:معبد ضريح شيزوكا سينجين]][[commons:Category:Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja]][[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]][[es:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[eu:Shizuoka Sengen santutegia]][[fr:Shizuoka Sengen-jinja]][[it:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[ja:静岡浅間神社]][[zh:靜岡淺間神社]] [[arz:معبد ضريح شيزوكا سينجين]][[sn:commons:Category:Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja]][[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]][[es:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[eu:Shizuoka Sengen santutegia]][[fr:Shizuoka Sengen-jinja]][[it:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[ja:静岡浅間神社]][[zh:靜岡淺間神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q500368]] Wikidata name: Shizuoka Sengen Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Shizuoka Sengen Jinja
静岡浅間神社 | image = Shizuoka Sengen Shrine Ohaiden.jpg | caption = Prayer Hall of Shizuoka Sengen Jinja | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|59|01|N|138|22|31|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Asama shrine | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Konohanasakuya-hime]],
[[Kamuo Ichihime|Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 102-1 Miyagasaki-chō, Aoi-ku, Shizuoka, 420-0868 | website = {{URL|http://www.shizuokasengen.net/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Shizuoka Sengen Jinja'''|静岡浅間神社}} is the name for a collective group of three [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s now forming a single religious corporation, located at Mount Shizuhata in [[:en:Aoi-ku, Shizuoka|Aoi-ku, Shizuoka]], in [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. These shrines are the Kanbe Jinja (神部神社), Sengen Jinja (浅間神社), and Ōtoshimioya Jinja (大歳御祖神社). The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 5. It is the [[Sōja shrine]] of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Kambe Jinja is the [[:en:Ookuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]], who is regarded as the mythical founding deity of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Sengen Jinja is the [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the deity of [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Ohtoshimioya Shrine is the Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto (大歳御祖命), who appears in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as a wife of [[:en:Susano-o|Susano-o]], and a ''kami'' protecting markets and commerce. ==History== The date of the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja's foundation is unknown. The area has been inhabited since prehistoric times, and a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]] has been excavated at [[Shizuhatagayama Kofun|Mount Shizuhata]]. Per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', the area was colonized by the [[:en:Hata clan|Hata clan]] during this period. According to unsubstantiated shrine legend, the foundation of the Kanbe Jinja dates to the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], that of the Ōtoshimioya Shrine to the reign of [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]], both from the Kofun period. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, Kanbe Jinja was given national recognition and status of the ''Sōja'' of Suruga Province in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. Also, the date of 901 is given for the foundation of the Sengen Jinja, as a subsidiary branch of the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], and initially was referred to as the "Shingu" (new shrine). Through the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura]] and [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]s, the shrines enjoyed the patronage of the powerful warrior clans who dominated the Suruga area: the [[Minamoto clan]], [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], [[Imagawa clan]], [[Takeda clan]] and the [[Tokugawa clan]]. In particular, the first Tokugawa ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'', [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]], sponsored the rebuilding of the shrines after his retirement to nearby [[:en:Sumpu Castle|Sumpu Castle]], and subsequent ''shōguns'' continued to worship at the shrines throughout the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. The 3rd ''shōgun'', [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]], granted the shrines lands with 2313 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'' in revenue for their upkeep. However, the shrine complex burned down in a fire of 1804. It was rebuilt over a 60-year period at a cost of over 100,000 gold ''[[:en:Ryō|Ryō]]'' by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in its flamboyant [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Momoyama style]], with extensive use of [[:en:Lacquer|Lacquer]], wood carvings, and [[:en:Gold leaf|Gold leaf]]. Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]], forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Shizuoka Sengen was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}. ==Subsidiary shrines== In addition to the three main shrines, the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja complex also has four subsidiary shrines: * Hayama Jinja (麓山神社) dedicated to [[Ōyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and to [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]]. It was founded in 1878 * Yachiho Jinja (八千戈神社), an amalgamation of 18 small shrines and 13 small chapels found in the surrounding area. It was founded in 1873. * Sukunahiko Jinja (少彦名神社), formerly the [[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi-do]] of Kambe Jinja, turned into a shrine in Meiji period due to [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Buddhism from Shinto]]. * Tamahoko Jinja (玉鉾神) dedicated to the four main Edo period ''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]]'' scholars. ==Cultural properties== Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]] (ICP), forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine's non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan}} * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]] ([[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]] [[Ichinomiya]]) ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons Category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). It is the [[Sōja shrine]] of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Kambe Jinja is the [[:en:Ookuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]], who is regarded as the mythical founding deity of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Ohtoshimioya Shrine is the Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto (大歳御祖命), who appears in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as a wife of [[:en:Susano-o|Susano-o]], and a ''kami'' protecting markets and commerce. ==History== The date of the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja's foundation is unknown. The area has been inhabited since prehistoric times, and a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]] has been excavated at [[Shizuhatagayama Kofun|Mount Shizuhata]]. Per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', the area was colonized by the [[:en:Hata clan|Hata clan]] during this period. According to unsubstantiated shrine legend, the foundation of the Kanbe Jinja dates to the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], that of the Ōtoshimioya Shrine to the reign of [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]], both from the Kofun period. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, Kanbe Jinja was given national recognition and status of the ''Sōja'' of Suruga Province in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. Also, the date of 901 is given for the foundation of the Sengen Jinja, as a subsidiary branch of the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], and initially was referred to as the "Shingu" (new shrine). Through the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura]] and [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]s, the shrines enjoyed the patronage of the powerful warrior clans who dominated the Suruga area: the [[Minamoto clan]], [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], [[Imagawa clan]], [[Takeda clan]] and the [[Tokugawa clan]]. In particular, the first Tokugawa ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'', [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]], sponsored the rebuilding of the shrines after his retirement to nearby [[:en:Sumpu Castle|Sumpu Castle]], and subsequent ''shōguns'' continued to worship at the shrines throughout the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. The 3rd ''shōgun'', [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]], granted the shrines lands with 2313 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'' in revenue for their upkeep. However, the shrine complex burned down in a fire of 1804. It was rebuilt over a 60-year period at a cost of over 100,000 gold ''[[:en:Ryō|Ryō]]'' by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in its flamboyant [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Momoyama style]], with extensive use of [[:en:Lacquer|Lacquer]], wood carvings, and [[:en:Gold leaf|Gold leaf]]. Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]], forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Shizuoka Sengen was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}. ==Subsidiary shrines== In addition to the three main shrines, the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja complex also has four subsidiary shrines: * Hayama Jinja (麓山神社) dedicated to [[Ōyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and to [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]]. It was founded in 1878 * Yachiho Jinja (八千戈神社), an amalgamation of 18 small shrines and 13 small chapels found in the surrounding area. It was founded in 1873. * Sukunahiko Jinja (少彦名神社), formerly the [[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi-do]] of Kambe Jinja, turned into a shrine in Meiji period due to [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Buddhism from Shinto]]. * Tamahoko Jinja (玉鉾神) dedicated to the four main Edo period ''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]]'' scholars. ==Cultural properties== Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]] (ICP), forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine's non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan}} * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]] ([[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]] [[Ichinomiya]]) ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons Category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. [[arz:معبد ضريح شيزوكا سينجين]][[co:commons:Category:Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja]][[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]][[es:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[eu:Shizuoka Sengen santutegia]][[fr:Shizuoka Sengen-jinja]][[it:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[ja:静岡浅間神社]][[zh:靜岡淺間神社]] [[arz:معبد ضريح شيزوكا سينجين]][[co:commons:Category:Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja]][[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]][[es:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[eu:Shizuoka Sengen santutegia]][[fr:Shizuoka Sengen-jinja]][[it:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[ja:静岡浅間神社]][[zh:靜岡淺間神社]] [[arz:معبد ضريح شيزوكا سينجين]][[sn:commons:Category:Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja]][[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]][[es:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[eu:Shizuoka Sengen santutegia]][[fr:Shizuoka Sengen-jinja]][[it:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[ja:静岡浅間神社]][[zh:靜岡淺間神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q500368]] Wikidata name: Shizuoka Sengen Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Shizuoka Sengen Jinja
静岡浅間神社 | image = Shizuoka Sengen Shrine Ohaiden.jpg | caption = Prayer Hall of Shizuoka Sengen Jinja | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|59|01|N|138|22|31|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Asama shrine | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Konohanasakuya-hime]],
[[Kamuo Ichihime|Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 102-1 Miyagasaki-chō, Aoi-ku, Shizuoka, 420-0868 | website = {{URL|http://www.shizuokasengen.net/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Shizuoka Sengen Jinja'''|静岡浅間神社}} is the name for a collective group of three [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s now forming a single religious corporation, located at Mount Shizuhata in [[:en:Aoi-ku, Shizuoka|Aoi-ku, Shizuoka]], in [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. These shrines are the Kanbe Jinja (神部神社), Sengen Jinja (浅間神社), and Ōtoshimioya Jinja (大歳御祖神社). The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 5. It is the [[Sōja shrine]] of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Kambe Jinja is the [[:en:Ookuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]], who is regarded as the mythical founding deity of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Sengen Jinja is the [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the deity of [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Ohtoshimioya Shrine is the Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto (大歳御祖命), who appears in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as a wife of [[:en:Susano-o|Susano-o]], and a ''kami'' protecting markets and commerce. ==History== The date of the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja's foundation is unknown. The area has been inhabited since prehistoric times, and a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]] has been excavated at [[Shizuhatagayama Kofun|Mount Shizuhata]]. Per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', the area was colonized by the [[:en:Hata clan|Hata clan]] during this period. According to unsubstantiated shrine legend, the foundation of the Kanbe Jinja dates to the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], that of the Ōtoshimioya Shrine to the reign of [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]], both from the Kofun period. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, Kanbe Jinja was given national recognition and status of the ''Sōja'' of Suruga Province in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. Also, the date of 901 is given for the foundation of the Sengen Jinja, as a subsidiary branch of the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], and initially was referred to as the "Shingu" (new shrine). Through the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura]] and [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]s, the shrines enjoyed the patronage of the powerful warrior clans who dominated the Suruga area: the [[Minamoto clan]], [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], [[Imagawa clan]], [[Takeda clan]] and the [[Tokugawa clan]]. In particular, the first Tokugawa ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'', [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]], sponsored the rebuilding of the shrines after his retirement to nearby [[:en:Sumpu Castle|Sumpu Castle]], and subsequent ''shōguns'' continued to worship at the shrines throughout the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. The 3rd ''shōgun'', [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]], granted the shrines lands with 2313 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'' in revenue for their upkeep. However, the shrine complex burned down in a fire of 1804. It was rebuilt over a 60-year period at a cost of over 100,000 gold ''[[:en:Ryō|Ryō]]'' by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in its flamboyant [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Momoyama style]], with extensive use of [[:en:Lacquer|Lacquer]], wood carvings, and [[:en:Gold leaf|Gold leaf]]. Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]], forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Shizuoka Sengen was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}. ==Subsidiary shrines== In addition to the three main shrines, the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja complex also has four subsidiary shrines: * Hayama Jinja (麓山神社) dedicated to [[Ōyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and to [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]]. It was founded in 1878 * Yachiho Jinja (八千戈神社), an amalgamation of 18 small shrines and 13 small chapels found in the surrounding area. It was founded in 1873. * Sukunahiko Jinja (少彦名神社), formerly the [[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi-do]] of Kambe Jinja, turned into a shrine in Meiji period due to [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Buddhism from Shinto]]. * Tamahoko Jinja (玉鉾神) dedicated to the four main Edo period ''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]]'' scholars. ==Cultural properties== Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]] (ICP), forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine's non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan}} * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]] ([[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]] [[Ichinomiya]]) ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons Category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} {{Asama Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Sōja shrines}} [[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] [[arz:معبد ضريح شيزوكا سينجين]] {{Commons category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} [[es:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]] [[eu:Shizuoka Sengen santutegia]] [[fr:Shizuoka Sengen-jinja]] [[it:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]] [[ja:静岡浅間神社]] [[zh:靜岡淺間神社]] {{wikidata link|Q500368}} [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shizuoka (city)]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, Kanbe Jinja was given national recognition and status of the ''Sōja'' of Suruga Province in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. Also, the date of 901 is given for the foundation of the Sengen Jinja, as a subsidiary branch of the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], and initially was referred to as the "Shingu" (new shrine). Through the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura}} and {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}s, the shrines enjoyed the patronage of the powerful warrior clans who dominated the Suruga area: the [[Minamoto clan]], {{ill|Hōjō clan|en|Hōjō clan|WD=Q834364|lt=Hōjō clan}}, [[Imagawa clan]], [[Takeda clan]] and the [[Tokugawa clan]]. In particular, the first Tokugawa ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'', {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, sponsored the rebuilding of the shrines after his retirement to nearby [[:en:Sumpu Castle|Sumpu Castle]], and subsequent ''shōguns'' continued to worship at the shrines throughout the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}. The 3rd ''shōgun'', {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|en|Tokugawa Iemitsu|WD=Q314481|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu}}, granted the shrines lands with 2313 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}'' in revenue for their upkeep. However, the shrine complex burned down in a fire of 1804. It was rebuilt over a 60-year period at a cost of over 100,000 gold ''{{ill|Ryō|en|Ryō|WD=Q3057942|lt=Ryō}}'' by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in its flamboyant {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Momoyama style}}, with extensive use of {{ill|Lacquer|en|Lacquer|WD=Q11236878|lt=Lacquer}}, wood carvings, and {{ill|Gold leaf|en|Gold leaf|WD=Q929186|lt=Gold leaf}}. Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties}}, forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Shizuoka Sengen was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}. ==Subsidiary shrines== In addition to the three main shrines, the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja complex also has four subsidiary shrines: * Hayama Jinja (麓山神社) dedicated to [[Ōyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}}. It was founded in 1878 * Yachiho Jinja (八千戈神社), an amalgamation of 18 small shrines and 13 small chapels found in the surrounding area. It was founded in 1873. * Sukunahiko Jinja (少彦名神社), formerly the [[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi-do]] of Kambe Jinja, turned into a shrine in Meiji period due to [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Buddhism from Shinto]]. * Tamahoko Jinja (玉鉾神) dedicated to the four main Edo period ''{{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}}'' scholars. ==Cultural properties== Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties}} (ICP), forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine's non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan}} * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} * [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]] ({{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province}} [[Ichinomiya]]) ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons Category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} {{Asama Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Sōja shrines}} [[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] [[arz:معبد ضريح شيزوكا سينجين]] {{Commons category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} [[es:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]] [[eu:Shizuoka Sengen santutegia]] [[fr:Shizuoka Sengen-jinja]] [[it:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]] [[ja:静岡浅間神社]] [[zh:靜岡淺間神社]] {{wikidata link|Q500368}} [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shizuoka (city)]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[arz:معبد ضريح شيزوكا سينجين]][[co:commons:Category:Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja]][[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]][[es:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[eu:Shizuoka Sengen santutegia]][[fr:Shizuoka Sengen-jinja]][[it:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[ja:静岡浅間神社]][[zh:靜岡淺間神社]] [[arz:معبد ضريح شيزوكا سينجين]][[co:commons:Category:Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja]][[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]][[es:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[eu:Shizuoka Sengen santutegia]][[fr:Shizuoka Sengen-jinja]][[it:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[ja:静岡浅間神社]][[zh:靜岡淺間神社]] [[arz:معبد ضريح شيزوكا سينجين]][[sn:commons:Category:Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja]][[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]][[es:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[eu:Shizuoka Sengen santutegia]][[fr:Shizuoka Sengen-jinja]][[it:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[ja:静岡浅間神社]][[zh:靜岡淺間神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q500368]] Wikidata name: Shizuoka Sengen Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Shizuoka Sengen Jinja
静岡浅間神社 | image = Shizuoka Sengen Shrine Ohaiden.jpg | caption = Prayer Hall of Shizuoka Sengen Jinja | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|59|01|N|138|22|31|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Asama shrine | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Konohanasakuya-hime]],
[[Kamuo Ichihime|Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 102-1 Miyagasaki-chō, Aoi-ku, Shizuoka, 420-0868 | website = {{URL|http://www.shizuokasengen.net/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Shizuoka Sengen Jinja'''|静岡浅間神社}} is the name for a collective group of three {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}s now forming a single religious corporation, located at Mount Shizuhata in {{ill|Aoi-ku, Shizuoka|en|Aoi-ku, Shizuoka|WD=Q1131483|lt=Aoi-ku, Shizuoka}}, in {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. These shrines are the Kanbe Jinja (神部神社), Sengen Jinja (浅間神社), and Ōtoshimioya Jinja (大歳御祖神社). The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 5. It is the [[Sōja shrine]] of {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province}}. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Kambe Jinja is the [[:en:Ookuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]], who is regarded as the mythical founding deity of {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province}}. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Sengen Jinja is the [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the deity of {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji}}. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Ohtoshimioya Shrine is the Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto (大歳御祖命), who appears in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as a wife of [[:en:Susano-o|Susano-o]], and a ''kami'' protecting markets and commerce. ==History== The date of the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja's foundation is unknown. The area has been inhabited since prehistoric times, and a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]] has been excavated at [[Shizuhatagayama Kofun|Mount Shizuhata]]. Per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', the area was colonized by the {{ill|Hata clan|en|Hata clan|WD=Q987290|lt=Hata clan}} during this period. According to unsubstantiated shrine legend, the foundation of the Kanbe Jinja dates to the reign of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}}, that of the Ōtoshimioya Shrine to the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin}}, both from the Kofun period. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, Kanbe Jinja was given national recognition and status of the ''Sōja'' of Suruga Province in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. Also, the date of 901 is given for the foundation of the Sengen Jinja, as a subsidiary branch of the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], and initially was referred to as the "Shingu" (new shrine). Through the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura}} and {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}s, the shrines enjoyed the patronage of the powerful warrior clans who dominated the Suruga area: the [[Minamoto clan]], {{ill|Hōjō clan|en|Hōjō clan|WD=Q834364|lt=Hōjō clan}}, [[Imagawa clan]], [[Takeda clan]] and the [[Tokugawa clan]]. In particular, the first Tokugawa ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'', {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, sponsored the rebuilding of the shrines after his retirement to nearby [[:en:Sumpu Castle|Sumpu Castle]], and subsequent ''shōguns'' continued to worship at the shrines throughout the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}. The 3rd ''shōgun'', {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|en|Tokugawa Iemitsu|WD=Q314481|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu}}, granted the shrines lands with 2313 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}'' in revenue for their upkeep. However, the shrine complex burned down in a fire of 1804. It was rebuilt over a 60-year period at a cost of over 100,000 gold ''{{ill|Ryō|en|Ryō|WD=Q3057942|lt=Ryō}}'' by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in its flamboyant {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Momoyama style}}, with extensive use of {{ill|Lacquer|en|Lacquer|WD=Q11236878|lt=Lacquer}}, wood carvings, and {{ill|Gold leaf|en|Gold leaf|WD=Q929186|lt=Gold leaf}}. Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties}}, forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Shizuoka Sengen was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}. ==Subsidiary shrines== In addition to the three main shrines, the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja complex also has four subsidiary shrines: * Hayama Jinja (麓山神社) dedicated to [[Ōyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}}. It was founded in 1878 * Yachiho Jinja (八千戈神社), an amalgamation of 18 small shrines and 13 small chapels found in the surrounding area. It was founded in 1873. * Sukunahiko Jinja (少彦名神社), formerly the [[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi-do]] of Kambe Jinja, turned into a shrine in Meiji period due to [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Buddhism from Shinto]]. * Tamahoko Jinja (玉鉾神) dedicated to the four main Edo period ''{{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}}'' scholars. ==Cultural properties== Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties}} (ICP), forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine's non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan}} * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} * [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]] ({{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province}} [[Ichinomiya]]) ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons Category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} {{Asama Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Sōja shrines}} [[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] [[arz:معبد ضريح شيزوكا سينجين]] {{Commons category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} [[es:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]] [[eu:Shizuoka Sengen santutegia]] [[fr:Shizuoka Sengen-jinja]] [[it:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]] [[ja:静岡浅間神社]] [[zh:靜岡淺間神社]] {{wikidata link|Q500368}} [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shizuoka (city)]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine's non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan}} * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} * [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]] ({{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province}} [[Ichinomiya]]) ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons Category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). revision.text:
[[arz:معبد ضريح شيزوكا سينجين]][[co:commons:Category:Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja]][[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]][[es:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[eu:Shizuoka Sengen santutegia]][[fr:Shizuoka Sengen-jinja]][[it:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[ja:静岡浅間神社]][[zh:靜岡淺間神社]] [[arz:معبد ضريح شيزوكا سينجين]][[co:commons:Category:Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja]][[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]][[es:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[eu:Shizuoka Sengen santutegia]][[fr:Shizuoka Sengen-jinja]][[it:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[ja:静岡浅間神社]][[zh:靜岡淺間神社]] [[arz:معبد ضريح شيزوكا سينجين]][[sn:commons:Category:Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja]][[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]][[es:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[eu:Shizuoka Sengen santutegia]][[fr:Shizuoka Sengen-jinja]][[it:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[ja:静岡浅間神社]][[zh:靜岡淺間神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q500368]] Wikidata name: Shizuoka Sengen Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Shizuoka Sengen Jinja
静岡浅間神社 | image = Shizuoka Sengen Shrine Ohaiden.jpg | caption = Prayer Hall of Shizuoka Sengen Jinja | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|59|01|N|138|22|31|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Asama shrine | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Konohanasakuya-hime]],
[[Kamuo Ichihime|Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 102-1 Miyagasaki-chō, Aoi-ku, Shizuoka, 420-0868 | website = {{URL|http://www.shizuokasengen.net/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Shizuoka Sengen Jinja'''|静岡浅間神社}} is the name for a collective group of three {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}s now forming a single religious corporation, located at Mount Shizuhata in {{ill|Aoi-ku, Shizuoka|en|Aoi-ku, Shizuoka|WD=Q1131483|lt=Aoi-ku, Shizuoka}}, in {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. These shrines are the Kanbe Jinja (神部神社), Sengen Jinja (浅間神社), and Ōtoshimioya Jinja (大歳御祖神社). The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 5. It is the [[Sōja shrine]] of {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province}}. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Kambe Jinja is the [[:en:Ookuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]], who is regarded as the mythical founding deity of {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province}}. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Sengen Jinja is the [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the deity of {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji}}. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Ohtoshimioya Shrine is the Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto (大歳御祖命), who appears in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as a wife of [[:en:Susano-o|Susano-o]], and a ''kami'' protecting markets and commerce. ==History== The date of the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja's foundation is unknown. The area has been inhabited since prehistoric times, and a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]] has been excavated at [[Shizuhatagayama Kofun|Mount Shizuhata]]. Per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', the area was colonized by the {{ill|Hata clan|en|Hata clan|WD=Q987290|lt=Hata clan}} during this period. According to unsubstantiated shrine legend, the foundation of the Kanbe Jinja dates to the reign of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}}, that of the Ōtoshimioya Shrine to the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin}}, both from the Kofun period. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, Kanbe Jinja was given national recognition and status of the ''Sōja'' of Suruga Province in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. Also, the date of 901 is given for the foundation of the Sengen Jinja, as a subsidiary branch of the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], and initially was referred to as the "Shingu" (new shrine). Through the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura}} and {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}s, the shrines enjoyed the patronage of the powerful warrior clans who dominated the Suruga area: the [[Minamoto clan]], {{ill|Hōjō clan|en|Hōjō clan|WD=Q834364|lt=Hōjō clan}}, [[Imagawa clan]], [[Takeda clan]] and the [[Tokugawa clan]]. In particular, the first Tokugawa ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'', {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, sponsored the rebuilding of the shrines after his retirement to nearby [[:en:Sumpu Castle|Sumpu Castle]], and subsequent ''shōguns'' continued to worship at the shrines throughout the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}. The 3rd ''shōgun'', {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|en|Tokugawa Iemitsu|WD=Q314481|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu}}, granted the shrines lands with 2313 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}'' in revenue for their upkeep. However, the shrine complex burned down in a fire of 1804. It was rebuilt over a 60-year period at a cost of over 100,000 gold ''{{ill|Ryō|en|Ryō|WD=Q3057942|lt=Ryō}}'' by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in its flamboyant {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Momoyama style}}, with extensive use of {{ill|Lacquer|en|Lacquer|WD=Q11236878|lt=Lacquer}}, wood carvings, and {{ill|Gold leaf|en|Gold leaf|WD=Q929186|lt=Gold leaf}}. Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties}}, forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Shizuoka Sengen was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}. ==Subsidiary shrines== In addition to the three main shrines, the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja complex also has four subsidiary shrines: * Hayama Jinja (麓山神社) dedicated to [[Ōyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}}. It was founded in 1878 * Yachiho Jinja (八千戈神社), an amalgamation of 18 small shrines and 13 small chapels found in the surrounding area. It was founded in 1873. * Sukunahiko Jinja (少彦名神社), formerly the [[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi-do]] of Kambe Jinja, turned into a shrine in Meiji period due to [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Buddhism from Shinto]]. * Tamahoko Jinja (玉鉾神) dedicated to the four main Edo period ''{{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}}'' scholars. ==Cultural properties== Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties}} (ICP), forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine's non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan}} * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} * [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]] ({{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province}} [[Ichinomiya]]) ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons Category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. [[Kamuo Ichihime|Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 102-1 Miyagasaki-chō, Aoi-ku, Shizuoka, 420-0868 | website = {{URL|http://www.shizuokasengen.net/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Shizuoka Sengen Jinja'''|静岡浅間神社}} is the name for a collective group of three {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}s now forming a single religious corporation, located at Mount Shizuhata in {{ill|Aoi-ku, Shizuoka|en|Aoi-ku, Shizuoka|WD=Q1131483|lt=Aoi-ku, Shizuoka}}, in {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. These shrines are the Kanbe Jinja (神部神社), Sengen Jinja (浅間神社), and Ōtoshimioya Jinja (大歳御祖神社). The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 5. It is the [[Sōja shrine]] of {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province}}. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Kambe Jinja is the [[:en:Ookuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]], who is regarded as the mythical founding deity of {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province}}. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Sengen Jinja is the [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the deity of {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji}}. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Ohtoshimioya Shrine is the Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto (大歳御祖命), who appears in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as a wife of [[:en:Susano-o|Susano-o]], and a ''kami'' protecting markets and commerce. ==History== The date of the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja's foundation is unknown. The area has been inhabited since prehistoric times, and a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]] has been excavated at [[Shizuhatagayama Kofun|Mount Shizuhata]]. Per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', the area was colonized by the {{ill|Hata clan|en|Hata clan|WD=Q987290|lt=Hata clan}} during this period. According to unsubstantiated shrine legend, the foundation of the Kanbe Jinja dates to the reign of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}}, that of the Ōtoshimioya Shrine to the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin}}, both from the Kofun period. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, Kanbe Jinja was given national recognition and status of the ''Sōja'' of Suruga Province in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. Also, the date of 901 is given for the foundation of the Sengen Jinja, as a subsidiary branch of the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], and initially was referred to as the "Shingu" (new shrine). Through the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura}} and {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}s, the shrines enjoyed the patronage of the powerful warrior clans who dominated the Suruga area: the [[Minamoto clan]], {{ill|Hōjō clan|en|Hōjō clan|WD=Q834364|lt=Hōjō clan}}, [[Imagawa clan]], [[Takeda clan]] and the [[Tokugawa clan]]. In particular, the first Tokugawa ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'', {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, sponsored the rebuilding of the shrines after his retirement to nearby [[:en:Sumpu Castle|Sumpu Castle]], and subsequent ''shōguns'' continued to worship at the shrines throughout the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}. The 3rd ''shōgun'', {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|en|Tokugawa Iemitsu|WD=Q314481|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu}}, granted the shrines lands with 2313 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}'' in revenue for their upkeep. However, the shrine complex burned down in a fire of 1804. It was rebuilt over a 60-year period at a cost of over 100,000 gold ''{{ill|Ryō|en|Ryō|WD=Q3057942|lt=Ryō}}'' by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in its flamboyant {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Momoyama style}}, with extensive use of {{ill|Lacquer|en|Lacquer|WD=Q11236878|lt=Lacquer}}, wood carvings, and {{ill|Gold leaf|en|Gold leaf|WD=Q929186|lt=Gold leaf}}. Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties}}, forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Shizuoka Sengen was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}. ==Subsidiary shrines== In addition to the three main shrines, the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja complex also has four subsidiary shrines: * Hayama Jinja (麓山神社) dedicated to [[Ōyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}}. It was founded in 1878 * Yachiho Jinja (八千戈神社), an amalgamation of 18 small shrines and 13 small chapels found in the surrounding area. It was founded in 1873. * Sukunahiko Jinja (少彦名神社), formerly the [[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi-do]] of Kambe Jinja, turned into a shrine in Meiji period due to [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Buddhism from Shinto]]. * Tamahoko Jinja (玉鉾神) dedicated to the four main Edo period ''{{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}}'' scholars. ==Cultural properties== Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties}} (ICP), forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine's non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan}} * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} * [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]] ({{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province}} [[Ichinomiya]]) ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons Category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} {{Asama Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Sōja shrines}} [[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] [[arz:معبد ضريح شيزوكا سينجين]] {{Commons category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} [[es:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]] [[eu:Shizuoka Sengen santutegia]] [[fr:Shizuoka Sengen-jinja]] [[it:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]] [[ja:静岡浅間神社]] [[zh:靜岡淺間神社]] {{wikidata link|Q500368}} [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shizuoka (city)]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

It was rebuilt over a 60-year period at a cost of over 100,000 gold ''{{ill|Ryō|en|Ryō|WD=Q3057942|lt=Ryō}}'' by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in its flamboyant {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Momoyama style}}, with extensive use of {{ill|Lacquer|en|Lacquer|WD=Q11236878|lt=Lacquer}}, wood carvings, and {{ill|Gold leaf|en|Gold leaf|WD=Q929186|lt=Gold leaf}}. Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties}}, forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Shizuoka Sengen was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}. ==Subsidiary shrines== In addition to the three main shrines, the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja complex also has four subsidiary shrines: * Hayama Jinja (麓山神社) dedicated to [[Ōyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}}. It was founded in 1878 * Yachiho Jinja (八千戈神社), an amalgamation of 18 small shrines and 13 small chapels found in the surrounding area. It was founded in 1873. * Sukunahiko Jinja (少彦名神社), formerly the [[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi-do]] of Kambe Jinja, turned into a shrine in Meiji period due to [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Buddhism from Shinto]]. * Tamahoko Jinja (玉鉾神) dedicated to the four main Edo period ''{{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}}'' scholars. ==Cultural properties== Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties}} (ICP), forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine's non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan}} * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} * [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]] ({{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province}} [[Ichinomiya]]) ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons Category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} {{Asama Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Sōja shrines}} [[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] [[arz:معبد ضريح شيزوكا سينجين]] {{Commons category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} [[es:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]] [[eu:Shizuoka Sengen santutegia]] [[fr:Shizuoka Sengen-jinja]] [[it:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]] [[ja:静岡浅間神社]] [[zh:靜岡淺間神社]] {{wikidata link|Q500368}} [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shizuoka (city)]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

[[arz:معبد ضريح شيزوكا سينجين]][[co:commons:Category:Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja]][[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]][[es:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[eu:Shizuoka Sengen santutegia]][[fr:Shizuoka Sengen-jinja]][[it:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[ja:静岡浅間神社]][[zh:靜岡淺間神社]] [[arz:معبد ضريح شيزوكا سينجين]][[co:commons:Category:Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja]][[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]][[es:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[eu:Shizuoka Sengen santutegia]][[fr:Shizuoka Sengen-jinja]][[it:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[ja:静岡浅間神社]][[zh:靜岡淺間神社]] [[arz:معبد ضريح شيزوكا سينجين]][[sn:commons:Category:Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja]][[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]][[es:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[eu:Shizuoka Sengen santutegia]][[fr:Shizuoka Sengen-jinja]][[it:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]][[ja:静岡浅間神社]][[zh:靜岡淺間神社]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Shizuoka Sengen Jinja
静岡浅間神社 | image = Shizuoka Sengen Shrine Ohaiden.jpg | caption = Prayer Hall of Shizuoka Sengen Jinja | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|59|01|N|138|22|31|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Asama shrine | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Konohanasakuya-hime]],
[[Kamuo Ichihime|Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 102-1 Miyagasaki-chō, Aoi-ku, Shizuoka, 420-0868 | website = {{URL|http://www.shizuokasengen.net/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Shizuoka Sengen Jinja'''|静岡浅間神社}} is the name for a collective group of three {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}s now forming a single religious corporation, located at Mount Shizuhata in {{ill|Aoi-ku, Shizuoka|en|Aoi-ku, Shizuoka|WD=Q1131483|lt=Aoi-ku, Shizuoka}}, in {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. These shrines are the Kanbe Jinja (神部神社), Sengen Jinja (浅間神社), and Ōtoshimioya Jinja (大歳御祖神社). The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 5. It is the [[Sōja shrine]] of {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province}}. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Kambe Jinja is the [[:en:Ookuninushi|Ohnamuchi-no-Mikoto]], who is regarded as the mythical founding deity of {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province}}. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Sengen Jinja is the [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the deity of {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji}}. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Ohtoshimioya Shrine is the Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto (大歳御祖命), who appears in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' as a wife of [[:en:Susano-o|Susano-o]], and a ''kami'' protecting markets and commerce. ==History== The date of the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja's foundation is unknown. The area has been inhabited since prehistoric times, and a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]] has been excavated at [[Shizuhatagayama Kofun|Mount Shizuhata]]. Per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', the area was colonized by the {{ill|Hata clan|en|Hata clan|WD=Q987290|lt=Hata clan}} during this period. According to unsubstantiated shrine legend, the foundation of the Kanbe Jinja dates to the reign of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}}, that of the Ōtoshimioya Shrine to the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin}}, both from the Kofun period. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, Kanbe Jinja was given national recognition and status of the ''Sōja'' of Suruga Province in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. Also, the date of 901 is given for the foundation of the Sengen Jinja, as a subsidiary branch of the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]], and initially was referred to as the "Shingu" (new shrine). Through the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura}} and {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}s, the shrines enjoyed the patronage of the powerful warrior clans who dominated the Suruga area: the [[Minamoto clan]], {{ill|Hōjō clan|en|Hōjō clan|WD=Q834364|lt=Hōjō clan}}, [[Imagawa clan]], [[Takeda clan]] and the [[Tokugawa clan]]. In particular, the first Tokugawa ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'', {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, sponsored the rebuilding of the shrines after his retirement to nearby [[:en:Sumpu Castle|Sumpu Castle]], and subsequent ''shōguns'' continued to worship at the shrines throughout the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}. The 3rd ''shōgun'', {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|en|Tokugawa Iemitsu|WD=Q314481|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu}}, granted the shrines lands with 2313 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}'' in revenue for their upkeep. However, the shrine complex burned down in a fire of 1804. It was rebuilt over a 60-year period at a cost of over 100,000 gold ''{{ill|Ryō|en|Ryō|WD=Q3057942|lt=Ryō}}'' by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in its flamboyant {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Momoyama style}}, with extensive use of {{ill|Lacquer|en|Lacquer|WD=Q11236878|lt=Lacquer}}, wood carvings, and {{ill|Gold leaf|en|Gold leaf|WD=Q929186|lt=Gold leaf}}. Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties}}, forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Shizuoka Sengen was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}. ==Subsidiary shrines== In addition to the three main shrines, the Shizuoka Sengen Jinja complex also has four subsidiary shrines: * Hayama Jinja (麓山神社) dedicated to [[Ōyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi-no-Mikoto]] and to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}}. It was founded in 1878 * Yachiho Jinja (八千戈神社), an amalgamation of 18 small shrines and 13 small chapels found in the surrounding area. It was founded in 1873. * Sukunahiko Jinja (少彦名神社), formerly the [[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi-do]] of Kambe Jinja, turned into a shrine in Meiji period due to [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Buddhism from Shinto]]. * Tamahoko Jinja (玉鉾神) dedicated to the four main Edo period ''{{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}}'' scholars. ==Cultural properties== Today, 26 structures in the shrine complex are protected by the national government as {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties}} (ICP), forming one of the largest such complexes in the country. The shrine has a small museum, which displays finds from the Shizuhatayama Kofun archaeological site, artifacts pertaining to Tokugawa Ieyasu and the history of the shrine, as well as the shrine's non-structural ICPs, including a Muromachi-period ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] and 17 diagrams of the shrine prior to its late Edo-period rebuilding. ==See also== {{Portal|Japan}} * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} * [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]] ({{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province}} [[Ichinomiya]]) ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons Category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} *''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} {{Asama Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Sōja shrines}} [[en:Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] [[arz:معبد ضريح شيزوكا سينجين]] {{Commons category|Shizuoka-Sengen-jinja}} [[es:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]] [[eu:Shizuoka Sengen santutegia]] [[fr:Shizuoka Sengen-jinja]] [[it:Santuario Shizuoka Sengen]] [[ja:静岡浅間神社]] [[zh:靜岡淺間神社]] {{wikidata link|Q500368}} [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shizuoka (city)]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]
